story:
The day I slayed my first dragon began like any other, the sun warm on my nose while the rest of me stayed tight and braced from the night’s too-real dreams of chasing tree-dwellers. I cracked an eye: no feathers, no fur, just my room. Father still slept; Mother’s warm dent in the sheets drew me to her side of the bed, even though I knew I shouldn’t. Then a clatter, a sharp shriek. I sprang to the door. Open, you damned door! I commanded. The gap at the bottom breathed in the scents of our life—Mother rushing, the Youngling’s tears. Something was wrong. I ordered the door again. It defied me. Father snored. I prepared my final charge when footsteps neared. A click—Mother appeared with the Youngling crying on her hip. I wanted to lick the salt from her cheeks, to defeat what hurt her, but Mother’s tone was tight. She shoved me into the living room, shut the barrier, set the Youngling down. The little one clamped her tiny arms around my neck and squealed in my ear. Release me, I commanded. I had to lick my way free, accidentally finding pancake sweetness on her lips. Mother snapped, “Stop, Wolf,” still not meeting my eyes, sweeping crumbs into her hand. No nose kiss, no belly rub, not even a pat. What is wrong, Mother?

The Youngling uproared a box, clattering its innards across the floor, and Mother’s voice cut the air. “Outside. Both of you.” We knew that voice. Tails tucked, we obeyed.

The yard reset my mind. Sun on my coat, scents spilling everywhere. I patrolled my perimeter, then claimed the oak. A blue bird glared down. Intruder! Intruder! I dug in until it fled. My house. All ordinary, except Mother’s edge. When I circled back, the Youngling dragged chalk across the patio. I sniffed her drawings; she pushed me off. I stayed close, nose to cracks and pots, passing ants, snails, roly-polies. Responsibilities. Priorities.

Then my whiskers twitched. Before I could study the new thing between two clay pots, the Youngling screamed. “Get it, Woofie!” I swept the ground—nothing. She pointed, fear sparking from her fingers. Sniff, sniff, sniff—then I saw it: still as gravel, scales like spilled paint—green, black, white—eyes like black pins, mouth a pink cavern hissing. Limbs tucked so tight it looked long and limbless. A real-life dragon.

Intruder! Intruder! I warned. It did not flee. I felt that locked-out feeling, the collar tightening, the door shutting. I did not know how to fight this. The Youngling trembled. I pressed my nose to her leg: I protect, Youngling. For her, I stepped forward—one paw, another—nose lowered. The dragon hissed again. I flinched. Back, dragon! I don’t want to hurt you! It slid closer, water over stone. No creature had defied me so calmly. Heat rose—from every pizza crust denied, from every no. Leave! Leave, you wretched beast! It only blinked.

The door swung. Pain exploded in my nose. The dragon’s jaws clamped fast. I shook, pawed, yelped. Mother’s voice cut through. The pressure vanished; the beast sailed, then vanished into the bushes. Its detached tail flipped on the stones, an eerie echo of life.

Victory, I announced, sniffing the twitching prize. The Youngling cried on Mother’s hip. “Shhh, it’s OK. It’s gone.” Mother kicked the tail away, cupped my face, checked my stinging nose. “Looks OK.” Ear scratches. Father appeared. I shoved my head into his knee—my gallant leader. Mother explained. The Youngling added, proud: “It bit him right on the nose! And its tail came off!” Father rubbed my belly. “Good boy, Beowulf! Did you protect our princess?” I wanted more thanks, but he was busy.

The day slid back into ordinary. Father ate. Mother softened. The Youngling stayed inside. I climbed the unmade bed, curled in the warm patch, nose throbbing less, and wished for dreams of feathers, grey fur, and maybe pancakes—no dragons today. I had done my duty. I had protected what was mine.
